findings.title: User-controlled command passed to stdio transport without sanitization
findings.description: The `command` and `args` fields from McpServerConfig are passed directly to Experimental_StdioMCPTransport which spawns a child process. These values originate from user input via McpServerConfigInput and are stored in SQLite, but there is zero sanitization or allowlisting. Any user (or code path) that can call addServer() can execute arbitrary system commands. The only validation is that the field is non-empty for stdio transport. This is a command injection vector if mcp-hub is ever exposed to untrusted input (e.g., via IPC, API endpoint, or UI form).
findings.suggestion: Add an allowlist of permitted commands (e.g., ['npx', 'node', 'python']) or validate command against a safe pattern. At minimum, validate that `command` does not contain shell metacharacters (;, |, &, $, backticks). Consider whether args should be restricted as well. Document the threat model: if only trusted admins can register servers, note that explicitly in code comments and enforce it at the API boundary.

findings.title: Package has no consumer -- entire mcp-hub is orphan code
findings.code_snippet:
export { McpHub } from './mcp-hub'
export type { ServerStatus } from './mcp-hub'
export { McpClientManager } from './mcp-client-manager'
export { McpServerStore } from './server-store'
export { createTransport } from './transport-factory'
findings.description: No package in the monorepo depends on @proj-airi/mcp-hub. A grep for '@proj-airi/mcp-hub' across all package.json files returns only the package's own package.json. The exports are not imported by any app (stage-tamagotchi, stage-web, server) or other package. Per CLAUDE.md orphan detection rules, code without a live entry point is dead-on-arrival.
findings.suggestion: Wire mcp-hub into at least one consumer before merging. For example: (1) Add @proj-airi/mcp-hub as a dependency of apps/stage-tamagotchi or apps/server, (2) Import McpHub in the Anima orchestrator or a new IPC handler, (3) Provide a minimal end-to-end path: UI button -> IPC -> McpHub.addServer() -> McpHub.connectServer() -> tools available to LLM. Even a single thin integration proves the package is reachable.

findings.title: McpServerConfig allows invalid transport/field combinations
findings.code_snippet:
export interface McpServerConfig {
  transport: TransportType
  command?: string
  args?: string[]
  url?: string
  headers?: Record<string, string>
}
findings.description: Encapsulation: 4/10, Expression: 3/10, Usefulness: 6/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 4.5). McpServerConfig and McpServerConfigInput use flat optional fields for transport-specific properties. When transport is 'stdio', command is required but typed as optional; when transport is 'sse' or 'http', url is required but typed as optional. The type permits illegal states such as { transport: 'stdio', url: 'http://example.com' } with no command, or { transport: 'http', command: '/bin/node' } with no url. Runtime validation in validateInput partially compensates but only for the add() path -- the type system itself provides no protection, and update() bypasses validation entirely.
findings.suggestion: Refactor into a discriminated union keyed on transport: type StdioServerConfig = { transport: 'stdio'; command: string; args?: string[]; url?: never }; type SseServerConfig = { transport: 'sse'; url: string; headers?: Record<string, string>; command?: never }; type HttpServerConfig = { transport: 'http'; url: string; headers?: Record<string, string>; command?: never }; type McpServerConfig = (StdioServerConfig | SseServerConfig | HttpServerConfig) & { id: string; name: string; enabled: boolean; createdAt: number; updatedAt: number }. Apply the same pattern to McpServerConfigInput.

findings.title: JSON.parse without try/catch on DB-sourced data in rowToConfig
findings.code_snippet:
args: row.args ? JSON.parse(row.args) : undefined,
url: row.url ?? undefined,
headers: row.headers ? JSON.parse(row.headers) : undefined,
findings.description: rowToConfig calls JSON.parse on args and headers columns read from SQLite. If stored data is corrupted or manually tampered with, JSON.parse throws a SyntaxError that will propagate unhandled from any read operation (getAll, getById, getEnabled). This is an I/O boundary where defensive parsing is required.
findings.suggestion: Wrap each JSON.parse in try/catch. On parse failure, either return a default value and log a warning with the row ID, or throw a typed error: `throw new Error(`Corrupted args for server ${row.id}`, { cause: err })`.

findings.title: update() skips all input validation, can persist invalid state
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 3/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 2/10 (Aggregate: 3.75). The update() method applies a partial merge via spread and filterUndefined() without re-validating the resulting config. A caller can change transport to 'stdio' without providing command, or change transport to 'http' while leaving url undefined. The add() path calls validateInput() but update() has no equivalent check. This can corrupt the database with configs that fail at connection time.
findings.suggestion: After merging partial onto existing, call validateInput() (or a new validateConfig() that checks the full merged object) before persisting. Better yet, with discriminated union types (see merged-003), the type system would catch most of these at compile time.

findings.title: Transport resource leak when createMCPClient fails in connect()
findings.description: If createTransport succeeds (spawning a stdio child process) but createMCPClient subsequently throws, the transport is never closed. For stdio transports this leaves an orphaned child process. The transport reference is lost since it was never stored in the clients map.
findings.suggestion: Wrap in try/catch. In the catch block, check if transport has a close/destroy method and call it before re-throwing: `try { const transport = createTransport(config); ... } catch (err) { if (transport && 'close' in transport) await transport.close(); throw new Error(`Failed to connect server ${config.id}`, { cause: err }); }`.

findings.title: connectEnabled() fails fast via Promise.all, leaving servers unconnected
findings.description: connectEnabled uses Promise.all, which rejects on the first failure. If one of N enabled servers fails to connect, the remaining servers may not complete their connection (their promises are abandoned). In a hub managing multiple MCP servers, one bad server should not prevent connecting to the rest. This is an operational error scenario that should be handled gracefully per CLAUDE.md error_handling.classification.
findings.suggestion: Use Promise.allSettled instead. Collect results, log failures with server IDs, connect successful ones, and return a summary: `const results = await Promise.allSettled(connections); const failures = results.filter(r => r.status === 'rejected'); if (failures.length) { /* log each with server context */ }`. Return a result type: `{ connected: string[], failed: Array<{ id: string, error: string }> }`.

findings.title: aggregateTools silently overwrites tools with duplicate names
findings.description: When multiple MCP servers expose tools with the same name (e.g., both have a 'search' tool), Object.assign silently overwrites the first with the second. The last server to be iterated wins. This is a correctness bug: the user registered both servers expecting both tools to be available, but one is silently dropped. The iteration order of Map is insertion order, making it deterministic but non-obvious.
findings.suggestion: Namespace tools by server: e.g., `serverName__toolName` or `serverId/toolName`. Alternatively, detect collisions and throw an error or return a diagnostic: `{ tools: Record<string, unknown>, conflicts: Array<{ name: string, servers: string[] }> }`. At minimum, log a warning when a tool name collision occurs.

findings.title: shutdown() leaks store if disconnectAll() throws
findings.description: If disconnectAll() throws (e.g., one client.close() rejects), store.close() is never called. The SQLite database connection is leaked. Shutdown is a cleanup path where all resources must be released regardless of individual failures.
findings.suggestion: Use try/finally to guarantee store cleanup: `async shutdown(): Promise<void> { try { await this.clientManager.disconnectAll() } finally { this.store.close() } }`. Additionally, disconnectAll itself should use Promise.allSettled (see merged-010).

findings.title: Promise.all in disconnectAll() aborts remaining disconnects on failure
findings.code_snippet:
const disconnections = Array.from(this.clients.keys()).map(id => this.disconnect(id))
await Promise.all(disconnections)
findings.description: disconnectAll uses Promise.all for cleanup. If one client.close() rejects, the remaining disconnect promises may not complete. This leaves stale entries in the clients Map and potentially orphaned transports/processes. Cleanup operations must be best-effort for all items.
findings.suggestion: Replace with Promise.allSettled: `const results = await Promise.allSettled(disconnections); const failures = results.filter(r => r.status === 'rejected'); for (const f of failures) { /* log failure context */ } this.clients.clear();`.

findings.title: McpClientManager stores connection state only in memory Map
findings.code_snippet: private clients: Map<string, McpClient> = new Map()
findings.description: The clients Map is the sole record of which servers are connected. On process crash or unexpected restart, this state is lost silently. The store persists server configs but not connection state. After restart, getServerStatus() reports all servers as 'disconnected' but there is no mechanism to detect stale stdio child processes that may still be running. Per CLAUDE.md: 'Business state in memory' is a forbidden pattern when it affects reliability.
findings.suggestion: This is partially mitigated by the fact that MCP client connections are inherently ephemeral (child processes die with the parent). However: (1) Add a startup routine that reconciles: call connectEnabled() automatically or provide a reconnect() method. (2) Consider persisting a 'last_connected_at' column in the SQLite table so the UI can show connection history. (3) Document that connection state is intentionally ephemeral and why.

findings.title: McpClientManager.getTools() error path untested
findings.description: McpClientManager.getTools() throws 'Server not connected' when called with an unknown server ID, but no test verifies this error path. The happy path (line 50-55 in test) calls getTools on a connected server, but the rejection branch is never exercised.
findings.suggestion: Add a test: `it('throws when getting tools for non-connected server', () => { await expect(manager.getTools('non-existent')).rejects.toThrow('Server not connected: non-existent') })` in the mcp-client-manager.test.ts file.

findings.title: McpHub.connectServer() with invalid ID untested
findings.description: McpHub.connectServer() throws 'MCP server not found' for non-existent IDs, but this error path has no test. The only connectServer test uses a valid ID from addServer. A user calling connectServer with a typo or stale ID would hit this branch.
findings.suggestion: Add test: `it('throws when connecting non-existent server', async () => { await expect(hub.connectServer('bad-id')).rejects.toThrow('MCP server not found: bad-id') })` in mcp-hub.test.ts.

findings.title: Near-identical sse/http cases in createTransport switch
findings.description: The 'sse' and 'http' cases are structurally identical: both guard on !config.url, both return { type, url, headers? }. The only difference is the type literal string. This is a near-duplicate block (~9 lines each, 18 total) that could be collapsed.

findings.title: McpTransport union lacks common discriminant property
findings.description: The McpTransport union mixes a class instance (Experimental_StdioMCPTransport, which has no 'type' property) with two plain object variants that do have a 'type' discriminant. This makes the union non-discriminated -- consumers cannot use a simple type guard to narrow the type. The resulting ergonomic penalty is visible at mcp-client-manager.ts:20 where an unsafe 'as McpTransport' cast is needed.
findings.suggestion: Wrap all three variants with a consistent discriminant: type McpTransport = { type: 'stdio'; transport: Experimental_StdioMCPTransport } | { type: 'sse'; url: string; headers?: Record<string, string> } | { type: 'http'; url: string; headers?: Record<string, string> }. This enables clean narrowing and eliminates the cast.

findings.title: rowToConfig crashes on malformed JSON in args/headers columns
findings.description: If the SQLite database contains malformed JSON in the args or headers columns (due to manual DB editing, migration corruption, or a bug in a future code path), JSON.parse will throw an unhandled exception. This would crash getAll(), getById(), getEnabled(), and update() -- making the entire store unusable until the corrupt row is manually fixed in the DB.
findings.suggestion: Wrap JSON.parse in a try-catch that either (1) returns a default value and logs a warning with the row ID, or (2) throws a typed error with context: `new Error('Corrupt JSON in args column for server ${row.id}: ${err.message}')`. The first approach is more resilient; the second is more explicit.

findings.title: No JSDoc on exported classes/functions (3 classes, 1 function)
findings.code_snippet:
export { McpHub } from './mcp-hub'
export { McpClientManager } from './mcp-client-manager'
export { McpServerStore } from './server-store'
export { createTransport } from './transport-factory'
findings.description: The mcp-hub package exports McpHub (12 public methods), McpClientManager (7 public methods), McpServerStore (7 public methods), and createTransport -- all without any JSDoc documentation. The project convention observed in other packages is to provide JSDoc on exported public APIs.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to each exported class and its public methods. At minimum, document McpHub (the primary public facade), including parameter descriptions, return types, and error conditions.

findings.title: Duplicate url/command validation in store and transport factory
findings.description: The same transport-specific validation (stdio requires command, sse/http requires url) is performed in both McpServerStore.validateInput (lines 142-158) and createTransport (lines 13, 24, 34). If the validation rules evolve, both must be updated in lockstep.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared validateTransportConfig(config) function. Call it from both store.add() and createTransport(). The transport-factory guards can remain as defensive checks with an explicit comment: '// Defensive: validated at store level'.

findings.title: McpHub.updateServer() and getServer() have no tests
findings.description: McpHub exposes updateServer() and getServer() as public API methods, but mcp-hub.test.ts has no tests for either. While the underlying store methods are tested separately, the hub-level delegation is never verified.
findings.suggestion: Add hub-level tests for updateServer (name change, enable/disable) and getServer (found, not found) in mcp-hub.test.ts. Even simple delegation tests catch future regressions when the hub layer adds logic.

findings.title: No Valibot schema validation at Imperative Shell boundary
findings.description: McpServerStore is an Imperative Shell boundary that accepts external input and persists it to SQLite. The project standard is Valibot for boundary validation, but this module uses manual if-checks. Manual validation is incomplete (does not validate name length, url format, transport enum membership) and fragile.
findings.suggestion: Define a Valibot schema for McpServerConfigInput (e.g., v.variant('transport', [stdioSchema, sseSchema, httpSchema])) and call v.parse() at the store boundary.

findings.title: removeServer leaves store entry if disconnect throws
findings.description: If clientManager.disconnect(id) throws, store.remove(id) is never reached. The server config remains in the database while the user believes the removal failed entirely. The store entry should still be removed even if disconnect fails.
findings.suggestion: Use try/finally or handle disconnect failure gracefully: `try { await this.clientManager.disconnect(id) } catch (err) { /* log warning: disconnect failed during removal */ } this.store.remove(id)`.

findings.title: Unsafe 'as' cast on DB row transport field bypasses type safety
findings.description: McpServerRow.transport is typed as plain 'string' (matching SQLite's TEXT column), and rowToConfig() casts it to TransportType with 'as'. If the DB contains a value not in the TransportType union, the cast silently produces an invalid McpServerConfig.
findings.suggestion: Add a runtime guard before casting: function isTransportType(s: string): s is TransportType { return ['stdio', 'sse', 'http'].includes(s) }. Throw an error if the DB value is not a valid transport.

findings.title: No structured logging anywhere in the package
findings.description: The entire mcp-hub package has zero logging. Server connections, disconnections, failures, tool aggregation, config changes -- none are logged. Per CLAUDE.md, structured logging is required for business events. MCP server lifecycle events are critical operational data.
findings.suggestion: Inject @guiiai/logg via useLogg() pattern (consistent with the rest of the monorepo). Log at INFO level: server added/removed/updated, connection established/closed. Log at WARN level: connection failures, tool name collisions. Log at ERROR level: store corruption, transport creation failures.

findings.title: update() skips transport-specific validation after merge
findings.description: The add() method calls validateInput() to ensure stdio has command and sse/http has url. But update() merges the partial input onto the existing config without re-validating the result. A user can update a stdio server to remove its command or change transport without providing required fields.
findings.suggestion: Call this.validateInput(updated) after the merge in update(). This ensures the invariant holds: every config in the database is valid for its transport type.

findings.title: aggregateTools/getTools return Record<string, unknown> losing all type info
findings.description: Both getTools() and aggregateTools() return Promise<Record<string, unknown>>, which erases all type information about MCP tool definitions. Consumers receive an untyped bag of unknowns. The @ai-sdk/mcp library likely provides a typed return from client.tools().
findings.suggestion: Use the return type from @ai-sdk/mcp's client.tools() directly (e.g., ReturnType<McpClient['tools']>) or define a McpToolSet type alias that preserves the SDK's type information.

findings.title: No JSDoc on exported interfaces defining the public contract
findings.code_snippet:
export type TransportType = 'stdio' | 'sse' | 'http'

export interface McpServerConfig {
  id: string
  name: string
  transport: TransportType
findings.description: McpServerConfig and McpServerConfigInput are the primary data contracts for the package, re-exported from index.ts. Neither interface has JSDoc explaining the purpose of fields, particularly the conditional requirements: 'command' is required when transport='stdio', 'url' is required when transport='sse' or 'http'.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to both interfaces and annotate conditionally-required fields with their transport-specific requirements.

findings.title: connectEnabled() partial failure behavior untested
findings.description: McpHub.connectEnabled() uses Promise.all() which rejects on first failure. No test verifies this partial-failure scenario. The current test only checks the happy path.
findings.suggestion: Add a test that registers two enabled servers -- one with a valid fixture and one with an invalid command. Verify that connectEnabled() rejects and that the failure behavior is documented.

findings.title: Echo fixture ignores tool parameters -- tool invocation untested
findings.description: The echo-mcp-server.ts fixture registers two tools but both ignore their input parameters entirely. No test actually invokes a tool via the MCP client (callTool), so the end-to-end tool execution path is never verified.
findings.suggestion: Enhance the fixture to accept parameters and add a test that calls a tool with specific inputs and verifies the response content.

findings.title: Timestamp fields use bare 'number' (recurring primitive obsession)
findings.code_snippet:
createdAt: number
  updatedAt: number
findings.description: createdAt and updatedAt are typed as bare 'number', a recurring pattern in this codebase. A branded type like 'EpochMs' would express intent and prevent accidental arithmetic with seconds-epoch values.
findings.suggestion: Define a branded type: type EpochMs = number & { readonly __brand: 'EpochMs' }. Apply across the codebase in a dedicated follow-up. For now, add a JSDoc annotation: /** Unix epoch milliseconds */.

findings.title: Transport factory returns heterogeneous union type with plain objects
findings.description: The McpTransport type is a union of a class instance (StdioMCPTransport) and two plain object literals for SSE and HTTP. The plain objects have no 'start'/'close' methods and no shared interface with the class instance. The cast on line 20 of mcp-client-manager.ts is a red flag for runtime type mismatch.
findings.suggestion: Verify whether @ai-sdk/mcp's createMCPClient actually accepts plain {type, url, headers} objects for SSE/HTTP. If so, document this. If not, use the appropriate AI SDK transport classes.

findings.title: Merge sse/http validation branches in validateInput
findings.description: Two consecutive if-statements check the exact same condition (!input.url) for two transport types that share the same requirement. These can be merged into a single conditional.
findings.suggestion: Merge into: if ((input.transport === 'sse' || input.transport === 'http') && !input.url) { throw new Error(`${input.transport} transport requires "url"`) }

findings.title: All errors are generic Error -- no typed error hierarchy
findings.code_snippet: throw new Error(`MCP server not found: ${id}`)
findings.description: All error throws across the 4 files use plain `new Error(message)`. Callers cannot programmatically distinguish between 'not found', 'already connected', 'transport failure', etc. without parsing error message strings.
findings.suggestion: Create typed error classes: McpServerNotFoundError, McpTransportError, McpConnectionError, McpStoreError. Alternatively, use a Result type for operations with expected failure modes.

findings.title: No test for duplicate server name behavior
findings.description: McpServerStore.add() does not enforce unique names, and no test documents whether duplicate names are intentionally allowed or accidentally permitted.
findings.suggestion: Add a test that explicitly adds two servers with the same name and asserts the expected behavior, documenting the design decision.

positive_observations: Server config is persisted in SQLite (better-sqlite3 with WAL mode) rather than in memory -- follows the 12-factor stateless principle correctly
Tests use real SQLite databases (temp files) and a real stdio MCP server fixture -- no mocks, no InMemoryRepository, no jest.fn(). Excellent adherence to testing rules
The echo-mcp-server.ts test fixture is a real MCP server using @modelcontextprotocol/sdk, not a fake -- integration tests prove actual MCP protocol round-trip

Clean separation of concerns: types.ts (domain), server-store.ts (persistence), transport-factory.ts (factory), mcp-client-manager.ts (connection lifecycle), mcp-hub.ts (facade). Each file has a clear single responsibility
Parameterized queries throughout server-store.ts -- no SQL string concatenation
Input validation in server-store.ts validateInput() catches transport-specific missing fields at the boundary
Good test coverage: persistence across reconnect tests prove the SQLite round-trip; boundary/invalid-input tests prove validation; end-to-end McpHub test proves the full lifecycle
No forbidden comment patterns (TODO/FIXME/HACK/PLACEHOLDER) found in any of the 6 files
Error messages in throw statements are clear and descriptive, serving as implicit documentation of preconditions
Code is well-structured and largely self-documenting with clear method names
Constructor in McpServerStore properly cleans up DB on init failure with try/catch + close + re-throw pattern
Transport factory has exhaustive switch with default case for unknown transport types
Zero mock violations in the test suite: real SQLite, real MCP server process, proper dev/prod parity
Persistence tests verify data survives close/reopen cycle
End-to-end test exercises the full register -> connect -> aggregate -> disconnect lifecycle
TransportType is a proper 3-value string literal union, not a plain string
McpClientManager properly encapsulates the clients Map as private with typed methods only
ServerStatus is a 2-value literal union rather than a boolean, enabling future states
Separation of McpServerConfig (persisted) from McpServerConfigInput (creation) follows command/query separation
All functions are short (< 40 lines), consistent use of early returns and guard clauses
Maximum nesting depth is 2 levels across all files, cyclomatic complexity stays low (1-5)
filterUndefined helper is a clean pure function extracted appropriately
